candidates.direction_name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
ranking_notes: The current branch is measurably slower than the accepted best and has not produced a clean scheduler win after two consecutive micro-retimes.
Restoring the exact `489574e` surface is the fastest way to recover the measurement anchor before reopening narrower exploit families.
The restore family is still open in the frontier and remains the highest-confidence next dispatch when the active surface drifts.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 37 only clawed back a few microseconds from round 36 and still finished above the accepted `489574e` anchor at 24.18427181 ms versus 24.16427231 ms. The live PTX hot-band kernel remains the runtime owner, but the measured source has drifted off the best-known surface by a pair of small scheduler-path edits that did not convert into a real win. The highest-value move now is to restore the exact accepted PTX surface on the current tree, re-measure it on the current harness state, and re-open the tighter exploit families only from that clean anchor instead of stacking more tweaks on top of a regressed branch.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Source drift away from the accepted PTX hot-band steady state, not DRAM saturation and not another immediate structural pivot.
candidates.risk: Low. The exact restore surface is already buildable and previously measured as the current accepted best, so this is primarily a search reset rather than a novel correctness risk.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus 24.18427181 ms and the accepted 24.16427231 ms anchor
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the exact restore does not recover the prior plateau cleanly or if the restored source unexpectedly fails build/correctness on the current tree.

candidates.direction_name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
ranking_notes: This family is still open in the frontier and already has near-best historical evidence on the same PTX surface.
It stays ranked behind restore because the current source is already off the accepted anchor and should not stack more drift first.
If restore recovers the anchor, this is the cleanest next A/B to keep the loop moving.
candidates.hypothesis: If the restore anchor is recovered, the next bounded reopen should target the remaining PTX control-path seam that is still open in the frontier: the prologue wait/commit window around the first two staged K tiles. Historical evidence says that exact branch landed near 24.17100811 ms and never became a hard failure; that makes it the best near-anchor exploit once the current drift is removed.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual cp.async wait-group timing and inner-loop control cadence in the PTX 128x128 hot-band microkernel after the restore anchor is back in place.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The PTX path is correctness-sensitive, and this family has already shown only marginal upside, so another reopen should stay bounded to one scheduler-facing change.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the restored anchor
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the reopen does not beat the restored anchor or if it replays a previously closed negative subvariant instead of a genuinely fresh wait/commit-window retime.

candidates.direction_name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
ranking_notes: The geometry pivot still represents the best available structural upside if the PTX microkernel remains trapped in hundredths-of-a-millisecond changes.
It stays ranked third because the current branch should first recover the accepted anchor and finish one tighter PTX A/B before spending another round on a high-risk pivot.
Keep this family live so the loop can escalate again once the narrow PTX surface has been re-grounded.
candidates.hypothesis: The PTX hot-band path is still capped around 48% tensor activity and 16.6% active warps, so the structural 256x128 pivot remains the strongest non-local branch in the live frontier. The family is still too regressed to rank above the restore and near-anchor PTX reopen, but it should stay in the top three because it is the cleanest in-tree geometry change with real upside if the narrow PTX loop keeps stalling.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Structural latency hiding and register/shared-memory amortization on the hot band, not one more tiny PTX-local ordering tweak.
candidates.risk: High. This branch changes the dominant hot-band geometry and has historically carried larger regression risk than the PTX-local families.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 pivot still lands in the high-regression regime or if it requires broader writer-ownership surgery than the bounded transplant plan allows.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
